summary: Joe Reis shares observations about data engineering trends in 2024, noting the field is maturing through tool abstraction, renewed focus on traditional 'enterprisey' practices like data modeling and governance, and increasing pressure on teams to demonstrate business value through FinOps. He sees this as both exciting (data engineering is finally recognized as critical) and boring (more traditional enterprise work), which he considers a positive sign of the field's maturation.
key_points: Most data engineers at non-Big Tech companies still do vanilla work like ETL/ELT and data warehousing, with many companies still struggling with BI let alone AI
Tool abstraction is accelerating, allowing engineers to focus on solving complex problems rather than managing low-level infrastructure
Traditional 'enterprisey' practices like data modeling and data management are making a comeback after being out of favor during the 2010s
FinOps and proving business value are becoming critical expectations for data teams
Data engineering lacks standardized training, leading to scattered skillsets and reactive firefighting on most teams
money_quotes: Most of these companies are still struggling to do BI, let alone AI.
The pendulum is swinging back in favor of these practices as organizations recognize their importance in maintaining data quality, consistency, and governance.
It's like staffing your engineering team with interns and junior-level engineers who are super convincing and often need their code reworked.
Engineers want to engineer. Clanking on a keyboard is a very tactile and cathartic experience for engineers.

2024 seems both exciting and boring for data engineers. It's exciting because data engineering is finally recognized as critical to making data initiatives succeed. It's boring because data engineering is moving toward 'enterprisey' work. These are great things.
key_insight: Data engineering is maturing as a field, marked by a return to traditional enterprise practices and a shift from reactive firefighting toward strategic business value creation.
